sources.name: TASVideos Game Resources — NES / Castlevania
sources.notes: Primary verified source for movement speeds, jump timer tables, whip mechanics, and invincibility frame count.

sources.name: Data Crystal — Castlevania (NES) Notes
sources.notes: ROM-level notes. Jump trajectory stored at ROM bytes 0x1002D–0x10037.

sources.name: NES Games Disassembly — cyneprepou4uk
sources.notes: Annotated 6502 disassembly repository including Castlevania source.

sources.name: Romhacking.net — Castlevania
sources.notes: Hacking tools and documents. Jump ROM address documentation sourced here.

notes: Castlevania has some of the most distinctive physics of the 8-bit era: NO air control once a jump is committed.
Knockback on hit is severe and sends Simon flying backward regardless of direction.
Jump follows a PRESET TRAJECTORY stored in ROM — not physics-based. Jump direction cannot be changed mid-air.
Staircase movement uses a separate movement mode at half speed.
Simon cannot jump or use his whip while on stairs.
The 'committed jump' design creates a fundamentally different skill expression than Mario — planning and positioning matter far more than reflex adjustment.

walk_speed.source: TASVideos: 'Horizontal speed (walking/jumping/knockback): one pixel per frame'
walk_speed.notes: Simon always moves at exactly 1 pixel per frame horizontally. There is no acceleration or run button — speed is constant from the first frame of movement. This gives Castlevania a mechanical, deliberate feel.

stair_speed.description: Moving on staircases at half the normal walk speed.
stair_speed.source: TASVideos: 'Stair climbing: Two times slower than walking or jumping'
stair_speed.notes: Stairs are diagonal — Simon moves both left/right and up/down at 0.5 px/frame. He cannot jump or whip on stairs.

ground_movement.notes: NO acceleration. Simon moves at constant 1 px/frame from the moment a direction is pressed. Stopping is also instant — no deceleration.

jump.source: Data Crystal Notes: 'The bytes contained at 0x1002D-0x10037 control the height/trajectory of the character's jump'
notes: Simon's jump does NOT follow a gravity simulation. The Y position follows a preset lookup table of Y offsets.
This means the jump arc is FIXED — the same shape every time, regardless of physics.
NO variable jump height: releasing the jump button has no effect on jump arc.
NO air control: the horizontal direction is locked at the moment of jump. Cannot change direction mid-air.
This is the single most distinctive physics design choice in Castlevania — every jump requires pre-commitment.
Jump ROM addresses differ in the Vs. Castlevania version: 0x10557–0x10561.

knockback_peak_y.source: TASVideos: 'Peak height on knockback: 14 Y coordinates higher than his original position'
knockback_peak_y.notes: When Simon is hit, knockback trajectory also follows a preset path. Peak is 14 pixels above ground.

jump_timer_after_landing_frames.description: Frames before Simon can act after landing (varies by landing height relative to jump origin)

special_cases.notes: Can whip at frame 27 of the fall

jump_timer_after_landing_frames.notes: Jump timer determines when Simon can attack, jump, or use items again. Higher landing = less wait. This creates the memorable 'stiff landing' feel.

knockback.description: When Simon takes damage, he is launched backward (away from the damage source) in a predetermined arc.

horizontal_speed.source: TASVideos: same 1 px/frame as walk speed

notes: Knockback is one of Castlevania's harshest gameplay elements — being knocked off a platform or into a pit is common.
Simon cannot cancel knockback by any input. He must ride it out.
Knockback into pits causes instant death regardless of remaining health.
The severity of knockback relative to walk speed (1 px/frame vs 1 px/frame) means recovery takes meaningful time.
Later Castlevania games (Rondo of Blood, Symphony of the Night) made knockback optional or reduced — this version's knockback is iconic and unforgiving.

whip.source: TASVideos: 'Attack animation duration: counts from 1 to 22 frames. Hit connection at frame 17. Control regained at frame 22.'
notes: The whip has a 22-frame animation during which Simon cannot move or jump.
The hit box is active at frame 17 — the whip connects near the end of the extension.
After frame 22, Simon can act normally.
Sub-weapons can be thrown while the whip animation plays, unlike in some later Castlevanias.
whip.direction: horizontal only while standing, diagonal on stairs is not possible

invincibility_frames.source: TASVideos: 'Invincibility: Duration after damage is nearly 100 frames. Countdown starts at 48 and goes down by one unit every two frames. Total duration ~96 frames.'

holy_water.notes: Thrown in arc, spawns fire on ground contact. Fire persists for several frames.

axe.notes: Thrown in a parabolic arc upward. Good for enemies above Simon.

cross.notes: Thrown horizontally, passes through enemies, bounces back.

knife.notes: Fast horizontal projectile. Fastest sub-weapon travel speed.

stopwatch.notes: Freezes all on-screen enemies for several seconds. No projectile.

sub_weapons.cost_hearts: Each sub-weapon use costs 1 heart (axe costs 3)

candle_destruction.notes: Whipping candles/torches destroys them and spawns items or hearts. This is the primary resource collection mechanic.

boss_mechanics.notes: Bosses at the end of each stage. Have distinct attack patterns. The whip is the primary tool but sub-weapons are often more efficient.

simon_standing.notes: Simon is 1 tile wide, 1.5 tiles tall. Hitbox approximately matches sprite.

hitboxes.notes: Hitboxes in Castlevania are less generous than SMB. Enemy projectiles have relatively precise hit detection.

design_notes_for_giants_drink: The committed jump is the defining mechanic. Every level in Castlevania is designed around the player knowing they cannot correct a jump mid-air. Pit placements, enemy positions, and platform sizes all assume this constraint.
The 22-frame whip animation means Simon cannot defend himself while attacking. Positioning BEFORE attacking matters — you cannot dodge after committing to the whip.
The preset jump trajectory means jump height is always exactly the same — this predictability is a feature, not a limitation. Players can memorize the arc.
Knockback severity is intentional difficulty design. The game expects the player to be knocked into pits occasionally — lives and continues are the buffer.
The 1 px/frame constant speed makes level-segment traversal highly predictable and measurable, enabling careful pattern-based play.
